description: A14FJ - Day 6, part 5: Trans-Earth Injection Burn and Getting Ready to Rest

tokens: The lunar excursion of Apollo 14 is about to come to an end, with the crew moving behind the Moon to light up the SPS engine for the Trans-Earth Injection burn.
If all goes according to the plan, they will soon see the Moon recede behind them with the start of their three-day coast home.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Editor's note:All transcript times are presented according to the GET update at 054:53:36 that saw the mission timer moved forward 40 minutes, 2.90 seconds.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: This is Apollo Control, 149 hours, 23 minutes Ground Elapsed Time.
2 minutes, 54 seconds until Acquisition of Signal assuming a nominal Trans-Earth Injection burn which should have taken place at 149:16 Ground Elapsed Time, approximately 8 minutes ago.
At the time of the burn the Command Service Module of Apollo 14 weighed 34,720 pounds.
The burn itself consumed more than 10,000 pounds of SPS propellants which brought the gross weight down to 24,646 pounds.
That divides out to about 68 pounds per second of propellant going out through the 20,000-pound thrust SPS engine.

The latest impact figures from the Flight Dynamics Officer for the Lunar Module ascent stage Antares, 3.420 south latitude, line 19.667 west longitude At a Ground Elapsed Time of 148 hours, 22 minutes, 25.4 seconds as the Antares struck the surface the communications engineer here in Mission Control commented that the LM's high gain antenna was sending out high bit rate data right up to the last.

Back to the propellant used in the Trans-Earth Injection burn, after the 10,000 pounds is used in this burn, which is the second largest burn required of this engine, in a lunar mission, there's 1,600 pounds of propellant left which is adequate for weather avoidance trans-Earth mid-course corrections.
We're still about 39 seconds away from acquisition.
We'll bring t - up the line a few moments before we have confirmation of AOS.
7 seconds Let's open the circuit and listen to the air to ground.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Should the weather at their designated landing area worsen during their coast, a midcourse correction can be performed to change their calculated landing point further away from the bad weather.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: They have now emerged from behind the Moon for the last time, and will be within continuous communications coverage of the MSFN again.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Reading you loud and clear, Houston.

Burn time was 1 second long: 2 plus 29; residuals before trimming, plus 0.6, plus 0.8, minus 0.1; residuals after trimming, plus 0.2, plus 0.8, minus 0.1; Delta-VC, minus 21.1; fuel, 25; oxygen, 24; unbalanced decreased 40.
transcript.speaker: Shepard

tokens: We're making like tourists with the cameras right now.
transcript.speaker: Shepard

tokens: The period immediately following the Trans-Earth Injection burn is filled with photographing the Moon.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: At the present time, the crew of Apollo 14 is making photographs of the lunar surface using 500 millimeter lens on the hand-held Hasselblad cameras Apparently, the lunar topographic camera which was planned for some high resolution photography on the going away portion here from the Moon...
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: We have some flight-plan changes for you.
The first of them, though, is not effective until about an hour from now; so, anytime anyone has some free time, I'll read them up.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: We'll catch them in a little bit, if you want.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: Apollo 14 velocity now 7,462 feet per second, 482 nautical miles above the surface of the Moon and continuing to increase as the trajectory loops out away from the Moon in a slingshot effect.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Their velocity is 2,274 m/s and their distance is 893 kilometers from the Moon.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: The high resolution photography with the Hycon camera apparently has been scrubbed because of the malfunction of this camera.
This was scheduled at 150 hours 30 minutes and would have yielded some of the highest resolution photography thus far of the lunar surface.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Gold Team Flight Dynamics Officer, Bill Stoval, has passed on to the Flight Director an estimate of the present trajectory.
The vacuum perigee at Earth on the present path of this Trans-Earth Injection burn would be 74 nautical miles.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: At present, their closest approach to the surface of the Earth would be 137 kilometers.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: 14 is standing by for the flight-plan update.
transcript.speaker: Shepard

tokens: Let's see, open the page to 150:30.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: We'd like you to do everything as shown on up to the Verb 48 there at 150:35, or so.
And then, we've got another procedure to - for one last-ditch try with the Hycon.

The theory behind this is that in a static attitude with the FMC turned off, even though the shutter is malfunctioning, they think they can get an image on the film and then compensate for any exposure - off-nominal exposure to the film by development afterwards.
The procedure is not too lengthy, but find some blank paper to write it on there.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Gordo, are you really serious about dragging up the Hycon, now?
We're just getting ready to go to sleep here, as soon as finish these handheld pictures.
transcript.speaker: Shepard

tokens: Hey, Gordon, a little - Hey, Gordon.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: See, we hadn't planned on it.
We've got it all stowed and everything all over it, since we hadn't planned on -on using it.
It's pretty tough to get to, now.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: I was instructed to mention it to you.
If you had any feelings about it, well, just forget the whole thing; and that's what we'll do - just scrub it.
So, after you change the DAP load there, we'd like you to do the procedures, as shown an hour later there at 151:30; go into PTC and presleep; and you're clear to hit the sack after that.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: I don't mean to say you have to wait until 151:30 to do it.
The procedures, as shown at that time, you can do those as soon as you're ready.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: We'll probably do them a little sooner than that, then.
transcript.speaker: Shepard

tokens: If you'll give us P00 and Accept, we'll give you a PTC REFSMMAT,
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: They are now updating the computer with the navigational data needed to switch the inertial reference frame to the PTC one, to be used for the duration of the coast home.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Okay, you got it, Gordon: P00 and Accept.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: At your convenience, we'd like the Noun 93 figures on that last P52, prior to TEI.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Okay, Houston, on the last 52: Noun 71, 24, 31; Noun 05, all zeros; Noun 93 is plus .018, minus .091, plus .050; GET torque, 146:58:25.
transcript.speaker: Shepard

tokens: This is Apollo Control at 149 hours 59 minutes Ground Elapsed Time.
Apollo 14 homeward bound, now 9,801 nautical miles out from the Moon.
Velocity coming toward Earth is 6,022 feet per second.
Still up live on air-to-ground until the crew goes to sleep for the night.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: We notice that you are maneuvering to the attitude listed after that P52 option 1, which might not work for P52.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: If you read, you might cage your BMAGs, go to Rate 2.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: If anybody reads, you're dragging your BMAGs.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Well, don't know if you been trying to call or not.
We've been hustling around on this storage, here.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: And, do our rates look good enough for spinup?
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: Stu, if you'll give us P00 and Accept, we'll get a state vector before we spin up, there.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Also, I guess we're going to have one for you to write down by hand.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: For your information, we got a - we got a monster midcourse 5 coming up at TEI plus 17 of 1 foot per second.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Gordon's sarcasm is warranted - the translunar injection was so accurate that only a very minute course correction of 1 f/s is required per their latest tracking of their course.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: You all sure do good work.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: We thought you'd all gone to sleep on us up there.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Well, we were working on that, but we didn't have any place to sleep.
So, we've been scurrying around trying to get things in some sort of order.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: Well, we want to power down your tired bodies, here, as soon as you can arrange it.
We have nothing at all programmed for about the next 12 hours.
You guys have been doing an outstanding job here in the last couple of days, and we appreciate it.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Okay, Deke, I'll pass that on.
I'm the only one on the loop right now.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: I'll have that PAD in a minute or two.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Stu, on the sleep, we won't wake you until - until an hour or so after the scheduled wake up time unless you're up sooner.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: And I got the state vector for you.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Okay, it is a state vector 71.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: The yaw jet's just about to fire; and so, we'll wait a little bit here, on the PTC spinup.
Just want to be sure you have all your - your urine dump complete before - before you do it.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Yes, why don't you hold off on that for a little bit and we'll - We're not quite ready to spin it up.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: Is your clean happy home still clean?' We haven't heard much comment about any microlunar samples floating around.
transcript.speaker: Slayton

tokens: Flight Crew Operations Division director and Al's old Mercury 7 colleague Deke Slayton has slipped into the CapCom line to chat with the crew.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: It's amazingly clean, Deke, Almost no dust at all.
The suits were a little dirty but vacuumed off, and we got almost zero in the Command Module.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: Stu Roosa only one with a headset on aboard the spacecraft, is presently setting up the spacecraft in the barbeque roll or passive thermal control as it's called in preparation for going to sleep for the night.
Present altitude above the Moon 6,746 nautical miles, velocity 4,806 feet per second.
We're going to leave the line up until the crew does sign off for the evening.
At 151 hours 39 minutes standing by this is Apollo Control.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: You guys aren't worrying about stowing the probe at the present time, are you?
transcript.speaker: Slayton

tokens: We're just getting a little chow and getting - getting squared away - sort of relaxing and looking at the full Moon coming -going away from us and so forth.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: Well, we got a nice exotic procedure to read up to you some time in the next couple of days on final stowage on that thing.
And we didn't want you worrying about it.
transcript.speaker: Slayton

tokens: No, we're - We put it in a resting place up in the tunnel.
We figured that'd be pretty good until we got back.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: Apollo 14 now showing a height above the Moon of 8,149 nautical miles, velocity 4,673 feet per second.

The crew presently is eating, and after they're through with their meal they will call the Mission Control Center here for the last minute preparations before the sleep period, which may be extended to about 163 hours elapsed time.
They will establish the PTC, passive thermal control or barbeque roll for the night.
And, until they sack out we'll leave the line up at 152 12.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: We're ready to spin up any time the rates look good to you, and I have some onboard readouts.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: Well, they're taking a check on it.
They want to look at it a minute or 2, Go ahead with the readouts.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Yes, they might have some rates, now; I just - maybe want to wait a little bit, but - Okay: Bat C, 37.0; pyro A, 37.2; pyro B, 37.2; RCS, 60, 58, 60, 6o.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: And, we'd like to remind you that - to be sure when we get the PTC going, to start - to go the -get the comm configured as shown in the checklist.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: And far as crew status, we're all okay; no medication.
And, you ready for an E-MOD dump?
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: Gordon makes a request for the erasable memory dump now.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Just to make sure we're all together, you want to use the Omni mode for comm?
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: Stu, would you be sure that that Waste Management Overboard Drain is closed tight.
We want to be sure that this one takes, so that we don't have to interrupt your sleep.
And, if it is, then you're Go for spinup.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Houston's concern is that any venting from the drain opening might disturb their PTC rotation and require the crew to be roused for reestablishing passive thermal control.
transcript.speaker: Commentary
